scope: The spec serve local webapp + /api/* over the index. Belongs here: the SSR pages; the /api/* route contracts; loopback-only binding. Does not belong here: the one-model-one-seam promise (→ SCHM). Boundary case: loopback-only stays a SERV promise even though the data model it serves is defined in SCHM.

requirements.statement: spec serve launches the local webapp over the derived index, bound to loopback only.
requirements.why: CLI + DB + local webapp shape; loopback is the T-1-01 security posture.

requirements.statement: spec serve exposes the documented /api/* read routes (coverage, report, requirements, query, resolve, relations, propagation), returning 400 — never 500 — on malformed input.
requirements.why: The webapp and any external client depend on these contracts; a 500 on bad FTS input would leak internals and break the loopback UI.

requirements.statement: The spec binary embeds the webapp assets at compile time, so spec serve renders the UI from the single binary with no external asset files (--probe boots, fetches /, and confirms the page renders).
requirements.why: If assets were read from disk at runtime, a distributed binary would serve a blank UI — the self-contained-binary promise breaks silently.

requirements.statement: The Setup page detects the scan mode from the mapped member rows with the canonical spec row excluded: members expanded from one workspace root render Monorepo, two or more sibling member repos render Platform, and a lone member renders Single repo.
requirements.why: The count-based check included the canonical spec-engine row and hardcoded the Monorepo tile off, so the first mapping view an adopter checks — including this repo's own dogfood — misstated the layout as a multi-repo platform.

requirements.statement: The webapp Coverage and Requirements pages list only non-Superseded, non-Retired requirements by default — a domain left with no listed requirement disappears with them — and a toggle link (?all=1) restores the full ledger; a listed requirement's version-history panel includes its superseded predecessors in either view.
requirements.why: Superseded history soon outnumbers the live contract; an unfiltered default buries what the platform must satisfy now under dead versions, and hiding lineage along with the rows would erase the audit trail the supersede model exists to keep.
